dailyJudgementSnippet: OpenAI narrowly edges out Microsoft Copilot as the least negative name on Hacker News for 2026-04-03, but it was a close call: OpenAI had a mixed day with both harsh criticism and some product/pricing praise [E1][E2][E4], while Anthropic drew the strongest sustained backlash around Claude Code limits, quality, and security issues [E5][E6][E7][E8].
winnerExplanation: OpenAI wins, but only narrowly. Its aggregate score was the least negative of the four, helped by some clearly positive discussion around Codex being preferred over Claude Code and a lower ChatGPT Business price [E2][E4]. That said, sentiment was still mixed overall, with notable negative posts framing OpenAI as declining and criticizing its conduct [E1][E3]. Because this was a close-call day, the win is better read as 'least bad' than strongly positive momentum. Microsoft Copilot finished very close behind OpenAI, with a similarly mild net-negative score. Evidence was mixed there too: one strong trustworthiness criticism [E13] was offset by several practical, mildly favorable comments about value and usability [E14][E15][E16]. However, Copilot had very few mentions, so OpenAI's edge came on a somewhat firmer discussion base. Google Gemini landed more negatively overall. The main drivers were product/support complaints, especially around Gemini Code Assist account-routing failures and NotebookLM citation UX limitations [E9][E10]. There were some positives, including regular workflow usage and praise for Gemma 4 locally [E11][E12], but they did not fully offset the negative thread tone. Anthropic was the clear laggard. It had by far the largest discussion volume and the strongest concentration of negative sentiment, centered on Claude Code rate limits, quality decline, privacy/security issues, and jailbreak framing [E5][E6][E7][E8]. On this date, Hacker News discussion around Anthropic was materially worse than the other three.

openai.judgementSnippet: Winner, but narrowly. OpenAI's day was mixed: positive product sentiment around Codex and pricing [E2][E4] was enough to offset, though not erase, strong negative commentary [E1][E3].

anthropic.judgementSnippet: Clear loser of the day. High-volume discussion was dominated by complaints about Claude Code limits, degraded quality, and security/privacy concerns [E5][E6][E7][E8].

google_gemini.judgementSnippet: Net negative, but on a much smaller sample. Backend/support frustration and NotebookLM UX criticism [E9][E10] outweighed lighter positives like daily-use mentions and Gemma 4 praise [E11][E12].

microsoft_copilot.judgementSnippet: Very close runner-up to OpenAI. Copilot had a mixed but only slightly negative day, with one strong criticism [E13] balanced by several mildly positive comments about cost, workflow, and utility [E14][E15][E16].

evidence.summary: The headline frames OpenAI as declining and losing investor favor, which is strongly negative toward the company.
Directly compares Codex favorably against Claude Code, praising its generosity and the lack of CLI lock-in.
The comment directly accuses OpenAI of "grooming these groups" and calls it "sick and perverse," which is a strong negative judgment of OpenAI's conduct and strategy.
The story announces a lower price for ChatGPT Business, which is a positive pricing change for OpenAI's product. No criticism is expressed.

Strong complaint about Claude Code being heavily rate-limited despite a $200/month plan, with the author saying they are being "gaslighted" and should move on. This is a direct negative judgment of Anthropic's coding product and its limits.

The post says Claude Code has had a 'significant drop in its quality of work' with 'sloppy mistakes' and 'brute forcing its way through problems,' which is a direct negative judgment of Anthropic's product/model quality.

The story alleges a serious security/privacy flaw in Claude Code, an Anthropic product, saying it writes sensitive data to disk. That is direct negative sentiment toward Anthropic’s product quality and safety.
The item title frames Claude 4.6 as jailbroken, which indicates a security/vulnerability problem attributed to Anthropic's model rather than a neutral mention.

The post says Google sells Gemini Code Assist but its backend OAuth/server flow misclassifies paying users, forces unwanted Enterprise routing, and leaves the user stuck in a 403 loop. It also criticizes Google One Support for closing the ticket and not escalating a known backend failure.

The quoted user praise explicitly says NotebookLM cannot open the PDF locations where citations appear, only text, and prefers Ubik for precision. This is a negative product UX judgment tied to the NotebookLM surface under Google Gemini.

The author lists Gemini among the CLI tools they use daily, which implies they find it useful enough to keep in regular use. There is no direct praise or criticism of Gemini’s quality, just inclusion in their personal workflow.
Direct praise of Google’s Gemma 4 release and a strong positive comparison of using Gemma 4 locally with Zed versus a cloud provider.
The title directly says Copilot 'shouldn't be trusted with anything important,' which is a strong negative judgment of the product's trustworthiness.
Positive about the Copilot experience and value: the user gets a lot done, does not hit the $10/month cap, and describes the workflow as reasonable.
The comment describes GitHub Copilot as a cheap way to use Claude models, with only factual notes about caps and billing mechanics; overall this is mildly favorable to Copilot as a surface product.
The commenter says they get GitHub Copilot Pro for free and use it as their fallback until it runs out, which implies it is a useful and acceptable option. The praise is mild and mostly practical.
